phases.summary: The group unanimously agreed on the input estimates, with all participants calculating a total annual budget of 26 billion KRW (200 fellows × 130 million KRW each), 200 fellowship slots, and a 30% regional quota (60 slots for regional institutions). Key themes included the policy's explicit stipulations and the goal of balanced national research capacity through regional allocation. No disagreements emerged, as all estimates and reasoning aligned with the stated policy context. The central tendency and range were identical across all predictions: total_annual_budget_krw = 26,000,000,000, fellowship_slots = 200, regional_quota_pct = 30.0.

phases.summary: The group discussed the fellowship's fixed annual selection of 200 fellows, with applications estimated between 900 and 1200, reflecting a competitive acceptance rate of 16.7% to 22.2%. Consensus emerged that the 30% regional quota and eligibility restrictions narrow the applicant pool, while midterm evaluations emphasize process-oriented performance, leading to pass rates of 80% to 90% (160-180 fellows). Disagreement centered on application volume, with lower estimates citing burdensome requirements and regional quota discouraging Seoul-based applicants, and on midterm pass rates, with lower estimates highlighting risks from lab changes and weaker regional research environments. Central tendencies were approximately 1100 applications and 175 fellows passing midterm.
phases.summary_ko: 최종 선정 인원은(는) 정책에 명시된 값으로 다섯 참여자의 이견이 없었습니다. ‘지원서 접수’은(는) 구민혁이 900건로 가장 낮게, 김보람이 1,200건로 가장 높게 보아 갈렸지만, 토의 끝에 900건로 수렴했습니다. 이 과정에서 류수빈, 구민혁의 이견(이상치)이 흡수되었습니다. ‘단계평가 통과’은(는) 구민혁이 160명로 가장 낮게, 김보람이 180명로 가장 높게 보아 갈렸지만, 토의 끝에 160명로 수렴했습니다. 이 과정에서 구민혁의 이견(이상치)이 흡수되었습니다. forward와 backward(상향식) 추정을 대조하면 지원서 접수(forward 900건 ↔ backward 600건, 합의도 67%), 단계평가 통과(forward 160명 ↔ backward 140명, 합의도 88%)에서 차이가 남아, 이 단계의 핵심 불확실성을 보여줍니다.

phases.summary: The group agreed that 200 fellows are active in the 2021-2022 window, based on the policy of annual selection. For stage_two_fellows, estimates ranged from 160 to 174, with the majority (three participants) converging on 174, reflecting an 87% pass rate from the Activities phase, while two participants proposed lower rates (85% and 80%) due to risks from lab changes and weaker regional research environments. For annual_reports_submitted, there was consensus on 400 reports, as only the first cohort of 200 fellows submits one report per year in the two-year window, with one outlier estimating 600 by incorrectly including the full cohort lifetime. Key causal mechanisms discussed included midterm evaluation pass rates, lab change disruptions, and regional infrastructure challenges affecting attrition.

phases.summary: The group focused on the early-stage challenges of the first cohort of 200 fellows, emphasizing that ramp-up time, lab changes (10-15% within 3 years), and weaker infrastructure at regional institutions (30% quota) would limit initial SCI(E) publication output. There was strong consensus on the regional participation rate (30%) and on a lower publication estimate of 340 (0.85 per fellow per year) and a 25% tenure-track transition rate, driven by competitive job markets and regional barriers. One dissenting voice estimated higher productivity (400 publications, 1 per fellow per year) and a 35% transition rate, assuming smoother early progress and policy prestige. Numeric predictions ranged from 340 to 400 for publications and 25% to 35% for tenure-track transition, with the majority converging on the lower end.

phases.summary: The group reached full consensus on all three long-term impact variables, estimating that approximately 20 former fellows (10% of the original 200) will hold tenure-track positions after 5–10 years, regional publication share will grow by 2.5 percentage points over a decade, and 30 former fellows will become independent PIs. Causal mechanisms centered on the 30% regional quota directing 60 fellows to regional institutions, where lower publication productivity (0.7 vs. 1.0 per year) and weaker infrastructure limit capacity growth, while the program's support for research independence enables non-tenure-track PI roles. All participants agreed that systemic barriers in the academic job market and regional research environments constrain the fellowship's long-term impact, with no disagreements in estimates or reasoning.

phases.summary: The group unanimously agreed on the input variables, with all participants estimating a total annual budget of 26 billion KRW (200 fellows × 130 million KRW per fellow), 200 fellowship slots, and a 30% regional quota (60 slots reserved). They consistently cited the policy's explicit figures for per-fellow funding (100 million KRW direct costs + 30 million KRW indirect costs) and slot allocation, leading to full consensus on all numeric predictions. No disagreements emerged, as participants treated these values as fixed policy parameters rather than estimates. The central tendency is identical across all predictions, with no range in values.

phases.summary: The group focused on the fellowship's fixed capacity of 200 selected fellows per year and the process-oriented midterm evaluation, which they expected to yield a 90% pass rate (180 out of 200). They estimated applications between 1,200 and 1,500, with a central tendency around 1,300–1,400, reflecting differing views on the eligible pool's size, including PhD candidates and regional applicants. All agreed on a 10% lab change rate during the first three years, citing policy allowances and typical mobility patterns. The main divergence was in application volume, driven by assumptions about competition and eligibility breadth.

phases.summary: The group discussed the early-stage productivity of 200 fellows in their first two years, focusing on publication output, tenure-track transition, and regional participation. There was consensus that the 30% regional quota is a binding policy mandate, and that the tenure-track transition rate is low (10-15%) due to the program's early stage. Disagreement centered on publication productivity: some estimated 1.2 papers per fellow per year (480 total) based on chemistry/pharma experience, while others argued for 1.0 (400 total) due to a setup phase in engineering and diverse fields. The range for publications was 400-480, with a central tendency around 400, and tenure-track rates ranged from 10-15%.

phases.summary: The group reached full consensus on all three impact estimates, projecting that 10% of the 200 fellows (20) will secure tenure-track positions, regional publication share will increase by 2.5 percentage points over a decade, and 15% of fellows (30) will become independent principal investigators. Key causal mechanisms included the competitive Korean academic job market, structural barriers limiting tenure-track positions, and the time needed for regional research capacity to grow despite a 30% regional quota. All participants reasoned that the fellowship's early stage (2021-2022) and modest cohort size constrained long-term impact, with no disagreement on numeric values or underlying logic.

phases.summary: The group discussed the fellowship's competitive selection process, with a fixed annual target of 200 fellows, and focused on how eligibility restrictions (domestic only, non-tenure-track, PhD within 7 years or under 39) and application barriers (host institution consultation) shape the applicant pool. There was consensus that the midterm evaluation is process-oriented and supportive, leading to high pass rates, and that lab changes are allowed and moderately common. Disagreement centered on the total number of applications, ranging from 800 to 1,200, with a central tendency around 1,050, and on the midterm pass rate, ranging from 80% to 90% (160-180 fellows). Lab change estimates varied from 10% to 20% of fellows.

phases.summary: The group focused on how the first cohort of 200 fellows (starting March 2021) drives outcomes in the 2021-2022 window, with the second cohort contributing only partially. There was consensus that midterm attrition reduces active fellows to about 170 and that the 30% regional quota is strictly enforced, though one participant estimated 28% due to applicant shortages. Disagreements centered on publication productivity per fellow-year (ranging from 0.6 to 1.5) and tenure-track transition rates (15% to 25%), with lower estimates reflecting regional constraints and longer engineering publication cycles, while higher estimates assumed policy support boosts output. Numeric predictions for total SCI(E) publications ranged from 320 to 480, with a central tendency around 360-400, and transition rates clustered near 20%.

phases.summary: The group discussed the long-term impact of the Sejong Science Fellowship on tenure-track transitions, regional publication share, and independent PI counts, focusing on causal mechanisms like the 30% regional quota, stable funding, and lab mobility. Consensus emerged that the independent PI count would exceed the tenure-track count due to non-tenure-track leadership roles, and that regional publication share growth would be modest. Disagreement centered on the magnitude of impact: some participants estimated higher outcomes (40 tenure-track, 2.5% regional growth, 50 PIs) based on policy support, while others predicted lower figures (20-30 tenure-track, 1.0-1.5% growth, 30-40 PIs) citing structural barriers for regional researchers and a competitive job market. Prediction ranges were: former_fellows_in_tenure_track 20-40 (central tendency ~30), regional_publication_share_growth 1.0-2.5% (~1.5%), independent_pi_count 30-50 (~40).

phases.summary: The group discussed the Sejong Science Fellowship's competitive application process, with estimates for applications_received ranging from 1,200 to 1,500, centering around 1,300-1,400, driven by the program's generous funding (130M KRW/year), 30% regional quota, and broad eligibility. There was strong consensus that fellows_selected is fixed at 200 and that midterm_evaluations_passed would be 180 (90% pass rate), due to the process-oriented stage evaluation focusing on faithful performance. Disagreement centered on application volume: some expected higher numbers due to the program's attractiveness, while others cited the rigorous selection process as a deterrent. All agreed that lab changes are permitted but rare (about 10%), with minimal impact on midterm outcomes.

phases.summary: The group focused on the active cohort math within the 2021-2022 window, with a clear consensus that only the first cohort of 200 fellows is fully active, as the second cohort starts in March 2022 with minimal activity. All participants agreed on fellows_active=200 and stage_two_fellows=180 (the expected 90% pass rate from the prior phase, though the midterm evaluation occurs after 2022). For annual_reports_submitted, the group converged on 600, calculated as 400 reports from the first cohort (200 fellows × 2 years) plus 200 reports from the second cohort (200 fellows × 1 year in 2022). One initial estimate of 960 was rejected as inconsistent with the window. The central tendency across all predictions is 200 for fellows_active, 180 for stage_two_fellows, and 600 for annual_reports_submitted.

Key causal mechanisms included the cohort size (200 fellows in the first cohort), the 25% tenure-track transition rate from the Outcomes phase, and the binding regional quota. No disagreements emerged, as all participants independently arrived at identical numeric predictions based on the same cohort math and policy parameters. The central tendency and range were identical: former_fellows_in_tenure_track = 50, regional_publication_share_growth = 5.0, independent_pi_count = 120.

The central tendency and range were identical across all estimates, reflecting complete alignment on these inputs.

phases.summary: The group discussed the fellowship's selection and midterm evaluation processes, focusing on application volume, selection competitiveness, and attrition due to lab changes and strict evaluation criteria. There was consensus that 200 fellows would be selected annually, as per policy, and that applications would range from 1,200 to 1,500, with a central tendency around 1,300-1,400. Midterm evaluation pass rates varied from 155 to 170, with a central estimate near 160-165, reflecting differing views on attrition from lab changes and process-oriented compliance. Key causal mechanisms included the 30% regional quota boosting applications, strict eligibility capping the pool, and lab changes potentially disrupting progress and lowering pass rates.
phases.summary_ko: 최종 선정 인원은(는) 정책에 명시된 값으로 다섯 참여자의 이견이 없었습니다. ‘단계평가 통과’은(는) 이은미이 155명로 가장 낮게, 한아진이 170명로 가장 높게 보아 갈렸지만, 토의 끝에 160명로 수렴했습니다. ‘지원서 접수’은(는) 한아진이 1,200건로 가장 낮게, 김창희이 1,500건로 가장 높게 보아 갈렸지만, 토의 끝에 1,300건로 수렴했습니다. 이 과정에서 김창희의 이견(이상치)이 흡수되었습니다. forward와 backward(상향식) 추정을 대조하면 단계평가 통과(forward 161명 ↔ backward 320명, 합의도 50%), 지원서 접수(forward 1,300건 ↔ backward 1,200건, 합의도 92%)에서 차이가 남아, 이 단계의 핵심 불확실성을 보여줍니다.

phases.summary: The group focused on the first cohort of 200 fellows starting in March 2021, with all active through 2021-2022. There was consensus on fellows_active (200) and stage_two_fellows (155-160, with most agreeing on 160 based on 20% attrition from lab changes and strict evaluation). For annual_reports_submitted, most agreed on 800 (2 reports per fellow per year: annual plus mid-year), but one participant estimated 920, citing potential extra reports from lab changes. The central tendency for stage_two_fellows is 160, and for reports is 800, with the disagreement reflecting differing assumptions about reporting frequency.

phases.summary: The group focused on the first cohort of 200 fellows active for about 1.8 years in 2021-2022, estimating an average of 1.5 SCI(E) publications per fellow per year. There was strong consensus on a tenure-track transition rate of 12.5% and full achievement of the 30% regional quota. Most participants adjusted the publication count downward from 540 to 480 due to lab changes and strict midterm evaluations, but one participant maintained the higher estimate of 540, arguing that stable funding and mentoring would sustain productivity. The range for SCI(E) publications was 480 to 540, with a central tendency around 480.

dissent.reason: This yields 200 fellows * 1.5 pubs/fellow/year * 1.8 years ≈ 540 publications. Therefore, I stick with my independent estimate of 540.

phases.summary: The group consistently estimated that 5-10 years post-fellowship, 25 former fellows would secure tenure-track positions (12.5% transition rate from 200 annual fellows), regional publication share would grow by 2.5 percentage points due to the 30% regional quota, and 40 former fellows would become independent PIs. All participants agreed these long-term outcomes would not be realized within the 2021-2022 window, as only the first cohort was active. No disagreements emerged; estimates were uniformly conservative, grounded in policy structure and typical career progression. The central tendency and range for all three variables were identical across participants: 25, 2.5%, and 40.

phases.summary: The group reached full consensus on all three input variables, estimating total funding at 410.6 billion KRW, 19,000 graduate scholarship slots, and 1,200 postdoc positions. All participants accepted the total budget as a fixed policy input. For scholarship slots, they reasoned that roughly 70% of the 355.2 billion KRW education research group budget (after operating costs and postdoc salaries) would fund stipends averaging 1 million KRW per month, yielding about 20,700 slots, but adjusted downward to 19,000 to reflect the active cohort of ~12,600 students and multi-year support. For postdocs, they assumed about 10% of the research group budget (35.5 billion KRW) at a minimum salary of 3 million KRW per month, producing ~986 positions, rounded to 1,200. No disagreements emerged, though some participants derived their estimates from budget breakdowns while others used group counts, and a few noted that Hanyang University's specific share was not the focus at this national-level phase.

phases.summary: The group reached full consensus on all activity estimates, citing the policy's explicit budget and schedule. Total funds disbursed were unanimously set at 408,080,000,000 KRW, based on the 70% March and 30% September installment plan. All participants agreed on 3 evaluation panels (annual, mid-term, compliance), 280 consulting sessions (the number of groups that opted in out of 578), and 205 compliance checks (targeting innovative talent training groups). No disagreements emerged, as each estimate was directly supported by the policy text and reinforced by cross-participant alignment. The central tendency for all four variables was a single value: 408,080,000,000 KRW, 3, 280, and 205 respectively.

phases.summary: The group reached full consensus on all output estimates, predicting 19,000 graduate students supported, 1,200 postdoc researchers, 57,000 publications, and 9,500 international conference presentations. They reasoned that these outputs flow directly from the established inputs and activities, with each graduate student producing about 3 publications per year and about 50% presenting at one international conference annually. The high compliance rate (575 out of 578 groups submitting reports) was cited as ensuring thorough documentation. No disagreements emerged, as all participants independently arrived at the same numbers based on typical productivity in humanities, social sciences, and engineering fields.

phases.summary: The group reached strong consensus on all outcome estimates, with unanimous predictions of an 86.3% employment rate, 15% international collaboration rate, and 82% full-time faculty lecture ratio. They reasoned that the program's solid research base (19,000 graduate students, 57,000 publications) supports these outcomes, but domestic industry focus limits international collaboration, and specialized courses still rely on part-time faculty. The employment rate slightly below the 87% policy target reflects a declining trend noted in the policy context. No disagreements emerged; all participants independently converged on the same values, citing similar causal mechanisms from inputs and outputs.

phases.summary: The group reached strong consensus that sustained BK21 investment over a decade will gradually improve South Korea's global S&T talent competitiveness rank from about 10th to 8th, and increase high-impact publications per 1000 researchers from roughly 10 to 12.5. All participants estimated identical values (rank 8, publications 12.5), with no disagreement. Key causal mechanisms discussed include the gradual accumulation of a critical mass of trained talent (19,000 graduate students and 1,200 postdocs annually), slow cultural shifts in research integrity and collaboration, and the time needed for alumni to reach leadership positions. The group emphasized that impact is not immediate and is tempered by international competition and the early-career stage of many researchers.

phases.summary: The group reached full consensus on all three input variables, anchoring estimates on the Ministry of Education's stated 2022 BK21 budget of 408,080 million KRW. They derived 19,000 graduate scholarship slots from the 355,151 million KRW education research group budget divided by average annual stipend costs, and 577 postdoc positions by assuming one postdoc per education research group (577 groups nationally). No disagreements emerged, as all participants applied the same resource logic and eligibility criteria from the policy context.

phases.summary: The group reached full consensus on all four activity-level variables, with every participant predicting the same values: total funds disbursed at 408,080,000,000 KRW, evaluation panels convened at 3, consulting sessions held at 280, and compliance checks executed at 205. Their reasoning was uniformly grounded in explicit policy documents, citing the 70%/30% installment schedule, three distinct panel types (annual, mid-term, compliance), the 280 consulting sessions offered to requesting groups, and the 205 innovative talent training groups targeted for compliance checks. No disagreements or alternative estimates emerged, reflecting a shared, process-driven interpretation of the policy context.

phases.summary: The group reached strong consensus that the Outputs phase directly follows from the Inputs and Activities phases, with all participants estimating 19,000 graduate students supported, 577 postdocs, 57,000 publications, and 9,500 international conference presentations. They consistently reasoned that typical graduate student productivity is 2-3 publications per year and postdoc productivity 3-4, yielding an average of 3 publications per participant, while international conference presentations were estimated at 0.5 per participant due to travel constraints. High reporting compliance (575 of 578 groups) was cited as ensuring reliable data capture. No disagreements emerged; all numeric predictions were identical across participants.

phases.summary: The group reached strong consensus on all outcome estimates, with every participant predicting a graduate employment rate of 78.9% (below the 86.7% target due to post-pandemic job market recovery) and an international collaboration rate of 50.0% (boosted by conference support but limited by domestic focus and barriers). The full-time faculty lecture ratio was uniformly estimated at 85.0%, reflecting policy emphasis on quality mentoring despite reliance on part-time experts for niche courses. Key causal mechanisms included network effects from research groups, training quality, and BK21 reputation for employment, while international collaboration was driven by co-authorship incentives and conference presentations (9,500) but constrained by language and visa issues. No disagreements emerged; all participants independently reasoned from the same prior-phase data (19,000 graduate slots, 577 postdoc positions, 408,080 million KRW funding, 575 of 578 groups compliant) and acknowledged that evaluation feedback loops (3 panels, 280 sessions) had moderate impact due to time lags.

phases.summary: The group consistently reasoned that sustained BK21 investment over a decade—funding 19,000 graduate slots and 577 postdoc positions, with high compliance and outputs of 57,000 publications and 9,500 international conference presentations—builds a critical mass of skilled researchers who enhance national innovation capacity. They agreed that dissemination of best practices via webzine and forums improves research culture, but that global competition limits South Korea's S&T talent competitiveness rank to a modest 12th, and the 2021-2022 time window restricts full realization of long-term effects on high-impact publications. All participants converged on identical numeric predictions: national S&T talent competitiveness rank of 12 and high-impact publications per 1000 researchers of 8.5, with no disagreement in estimates or reasoning.

phases.summary: The group reached strong consensus on all three input variables, treating the total budget of 410,597 million KRW as ground truth from the policy document. They consistently estimated 19,000 graduate scholarship slots nationally by assuming roughly 70% of the education research group budget (355,151 million KRW) goes to stipends, dividing by an average annual stipend of 12 million KRW, and adjusting downward from a raw calculation of ~20,700 slots. For postdoc positions, they uniformly estimated 1,000 nationally by allocating about 10% of the project budget to postdoc salaries at 36 million KRW per year, yielding ~986 and rounding up. No disagreements emerged; all participants anchored their reasoning on the same policy figures and allocation assumptions.

phases.summary: The group reached full consensus that the total funds disbursed equal the allocated budget of 410,597 million KRW, disbursed via a two-installment schedule (70% in March, 30% in September) to provide early liquidity for stipends and operations while allowing mid-year adjustments. All participants agreed on three evaluation panels convened (annual, mid-term, compliance), 280 consulting sessions held (as explicitly stated for groups that requested them), and 205 compliance checks executed (targeting innovative talent training groups). No disagreements emerged, as all estimates were directly derived from the policy document. The central tendency for all four variables is identical across participants: total_funds_disbursed = 410,597,000,000 KRW, evaluation_panels_convened = 3, consulting_sessions_held = 280, compliance_checks_executed = 205.

phases.summary: The group reached full consensus on all output estimates, with every participant predicting 19,000 graduate students supported, 1,000 postdoc researchers supported, 57,000 publications, and 7,600 international conference presentations. The causal reasoning was uniform: they derived the supported counts from the policy's stated 19,000 graduate slots and 1,000 postdoc positions, then applied a productivity rate of 3 publications per supported researcher per year (based on a typical 2-4 range in engineering/science) and 0.4 international conference presentations per researcher per year (reflecting a focus on domestic conferences and journal submissions). The high compliance rate of 575 out of 578 groups submitting reports was cited as ensuring reliable reporting. No disagreements or alternative estimates were presented.

phases.summary: The group reached full consensus on all outcome estimates, predicting a 90% graduate employment rate, a 20% international collaboration rate, and an 85% full-time faculty lecture ratio, yielding 17,100 employed graduates and 3,800 internationally collaborating participants. Key causal mechanisms cited included the program's strong reputation, network effects, training quality, conference support, and incentives for collaboration, tempered by a tendency for domestic focus and the continued presence of part-time lecturers. The high compliance rate (575 of 578 groups) was seen as ensuring reliable reporting. No disagreements emerged, with all six participants providing identical numeric predictions.

phases.summary: The group unanimously agreed that sustained BK21 investment over a decade will enhance national innovation capacity by creating a critical mass of highly skilled researchers, with dissemination of achievements and systemic cultural changes (e.g., mandatory ethics education, collaboration incentives) raising the overall S&T ecosystem's performance. All participants estimated South Korea's global S&T talent competitiveness rank at 8th (improving from ~10th) and high-impact publications per 1000 researchers at 12.5, based on 57,000 publications, ~0.5% top-cited rate, and 20,000 researchers, adjusted downward from 14.25 due to inactive researchers and engineering field norms. No disagreements emerged; all five participants provided identical numeric predictions and aligned reasoning.

phases.summary: The group focused on translating national BK21 budget figures to Hanyang University-specific inputs, using proportional allocation based on the number of education research groups. There was strong consensus on Hanyang's total funding (20 billion KRW), graduate scholarship slots (658), and postdoc positions (20), derived from the national total of 408,080 million KRW for 577 groups and policy constraints on stipend levels and operating costs. One participant initially provided national-level estimates, but the group converged on Hanyang-specific numbers. The central tendency for all three variables was consistent across participants, with no significant disagreement on the final estimates.

phases.summary: The group focused on the practical execution of funding and oversight mechanisms, with a consensus that the full 20 billion KRW would be disbursed via the 70% March/30% September schedule and that 3 formal evaluation panel meetings would occur. A key disagreement emerged over the scale of consulting sessions and compliance checks: one participant used national totals (280 consulting sessions, 205 compliance checks) while the majority scaled these down proportionally to Hanyang's ~20-30 groups, estimating 10 consulting sessions and 5 compliance checks. The central tendency for consulting sessions was 10 (range 10-280) and for compliance checks was 5 (range 5-205), reflecting the majority view that national figures were not Hanyang-specific.

phases.summary: The group discussed outputs as direct results of prior inputs and activities, focusing on graduate student and postdoc productivity. There was consensus on the number of graduate students supported (658) and postdoc researchers supported (20). Disagreement centered on per-capita publication and presentation rates: some assumed higher productivity (3 publications per graduate student, 1 presentation each) leading to 1974 publications and 658 presentations, while others used lower averages (2 publications per graduate student, half presenting) yielding 1316 publications and 329 presentations. The central tendency for publications was around 1316-1974, and for presentations 329-658, reflecting differing views on student output and resource constraints.
phases.summary_ko: 지원 대학원생 · 지원 신진연구인력은(는) 정책에 명시된 값으로 다섯 참여자의 이견이 없었습니다. ‘국제학회 발표’은(는) 전민성이 329건로 가장 낮게, 김찬영이 658건로 가장 높게 보아 갈렸지만, 토의 끝에 329건로 수렴했습니다. 이 과정에서 김찬영, 양세정의 이견(이상치)이 흡수되었습니다. ‘논문 산출’은(는) 전민성이 1,316편로 가장 낮게, 김찬영이 1,974편로 가장 높게 보아 갈렸지만, 토의 끝에 1,316편로 수렴했습니다. 이 과정에서 김찬영, 양세정의 이견(이상치)이 흡수되었습니다. forward와 backward(상향식) 추정을 대조하면 지원 대학원생(forward 658명 ↔ backward 200명, 합의도 30%), 국제학회 발표(forward 329건 ↔ backward 120건, 합의도 36%), 지원 신진연구인력(forward 20명 ↔ backward 50명, 합의도 40%), 논문 산출(forward 1,316편 ↔ backward 600편, 합의도 46%)에서 차이가 남아, 이 단계의 핵심 불확실성을 보여줍니다.
phases.basis: The group discussed outputs as direct results of prior inputs and activities, focusing on graduate student and postdoc productivity. There was consensus on the number of graduate students supported (658) and postdoc researchers supported (20).

phases.summary: The group reached strong consensus that the graduate employment rate would be 90% (592 out of 658 students) and the international collaboration rate would be 50% (329 students), with a full-time faculty lecture ratio of 85%. These estimates were consistently derived from prior phases showing 658 graduate slots and 329 international conference presentations, and were justified by the program's reputation, network effects, practical training, and incentives for full-time faculty hiring. No disagreements emerged, as all participants independently converged on identical numeric predictions. The central tendency across all estimates was uniform, with no variation in predicted values.

phases.summary: The group discussed that sustained BK21 investment over a decade builds a strong research culture and talent pipeline, but its national impact is gradual due to limited program scale and time needed for systemic change. There was consensus that Korea's S&T talent competitiveness rank would improve modestly, with most estimating rank 15, and high-impact publications per 1000 researchers at 35. One participant estimated a more optimistic rank of 12 and 45 publications, citing strong group-level outputs and dissemination effects, but others argued this overestimates near-term national impact given that many graduates are early-career. The numeric range for rank was 12-15, and for publications 35-45, with a central tendency around rank 15 and 35 publications.

agents.persona: 임소영 씨는 세종시 교육청에서 보건 복지 전공 지식을 활용해 복지 사각지대 학생들을 위한 심리 방역 체계를 설계하며, 복잡한 교육 법령 속에서도 핵심 지침을 빠르게 찾아내 학교 현장에 적용하는 능력이 탁월합니다.

agents.name: 박세린
agents.persona: 박세린 씨는 창원 국가산단 내의 거친 금속 공장 분위기를 부드럽게 녹여내는 교육 담당자입니다.

agents.name: 김은빈
agents.persona: 김은빈 씨는 수천 장의 자기소개서 속에서 지원자의 핵심 역량을 단번에 포착하는 예리한 통찰력을 가졌으며, 입시 전형의 복잡한 일정을 분 단위로 관리하는 꼼꼼한 입학 사정관입니다.

agents.name: 고기련
agents.persona: 고기련 씨는 교육학적 관점을 공학에 접목해 복잡한 제조 공정 설계를 쉽게 풀어내는 능력을 갖춘 교수이며, 학과 내 얽힌 행정 문제를 빈틈없이 처리해 동료들 사이에서 해결사로 통합니다.

phases.summary: The group reached full consensus on all three input variables, estimating total funding allocated at 408,080,000,000 KRW, graduate scholarship slots at 19,000, and postdoc positions at 577. All participants anchored their estimates on the fixed Ministry of Education budget and policy documents, with reasoning converging on the stated total of 12,600 future talent and 6,400 innovation talent students summing to 19,000 slots, and 577 education research groups each supporting one postdoc. No disagreements emerged; the central tendency matched the unanimous predictions.

phases.summary: All participants agreed on the activities phase estimates, citing the fixed 2022 budget of 408,080,000,000 KRW disbursed in two installments (70% in March, 30% in September), three formal evaluation panels (annual, mid-term preparation, compliance checks), 280 consulting sessions (voluntary participation from 578 groups), and 205 compliance checks targeting innovative talent training groups. No disagreements emerged, as the policy context and operational data were clear and consistent across all stakeholder perspectives.

phases.summary: The group agreed that the number of graduate students supported (19,000) and postdocs supported (577) are fixed by policy. For publications, there was a split: one participant estimated 38,000 (2 per participant), while the majority estimated 28,500 (1.5 per participant), citing that many students are early-stage and not all research leads to immediate publication. For international conference presentations, estimates were 7,600 (20% of 38,000) versus 5,700 (20% of 28,500), with the majority citing budget and travel constraints. The central tendency for publications was 28,500 and for presentations 5,700.
phases.summary_ko: 지원 신진연구인력 · 지원 대학원생은(는) 정책에 명시된 값으로 다섯 참여자의 이견이 없었습니다. ‘논문 산출’은(는) 박세린이 28,500편로 가장 낮게, 남성재이 38,000편로 가장 높게 보아 갈렸지만, 토의 끝에 28,500편로 수렴했습니다. 이 과정에서 남성재의 이견(이상치)이 흡수되었습니다. ‘국제학회 발표’은(는) 박세린이 5,700건로 가장 낮게, 남성재이 7,600건로 가장 높게 보아 갈렸지만, 토의 끝에 5,700건로 수렴했습니다. 이 과정에서 남성재의 이견(이상치)이 흡수되었습니다. forward와 backward(상향식) 추정을 대조하면 지원 신진연구인력(forward 577명 ↔ backward 3,000명, 합의도 19%), 논문 산출(forward 28,500편 ↔ backward 38,000편, 합의도 75%)에서 차이가 남아, 이 단계의 핵심 불확실성을 보여줍니다.

phases.summary: The group reached full consensus on all outcome estimates, predicting a graduate employment rate of 87.0% (16,530 out of 19,000 graduates) and an international research collaboration rate of 16% (3,040 out of 19,000 participants), with a full-time faculty lecture ratio of 75%. Key causal mechanisms discussed included the policy's target of a 0.3%p annual employment rate increase from an 86.7% baseline, and the conversion of 5,700 international conference presentations into sustained collaborations at roughly 16%. Participants agreed that evaluation and consulting feedback loops had limited impact in the first year, leading to conservative estimates. No disagreements emerged, with all estimates and reasoning closely aligned.

phases.summary: The group reached full consensus that sustained BK21 investment (408 billion KRW, 19,000 graduate slots, 577 postdocs) will gradually improve South Korea's S&T talent competitiveness rank from about 10th to 8th, and increase high-impact publications to 12.5 per 1000 researchers. Key causal mechanisms included the program's emphasis on research integrity, collaboration, and dissemination of best practices through forums and publications, which foster a healthier research culture and raise ecosystem performance over a decade. All five participants estimated identical values (rank 8, publications 12.5), reflecting strong alignment on conservative projections given the time lag for full effects. No disagreements were expressed; the reasoning consistently cited the program's scale, focus on quality, and gradual influence on national innovation capacity.
